dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for heart problems at 1012 Broad Street.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT here. How long has your heart been beating fast?
About twenty minutes now, since I woke up this morning.
Can you tell me your name and today's date?
My name is Marjorie, and the date is... September 7th?
Good. What city are we in right now, Marjorie?
We're in [CITY], I think. Is my heart going to be alright here, by the way?
Marjorie, let's verify your orientation. I'd like you to tell me the current month, the city we're in, and the reason we're here today.
It's September 2020, and we're in [CITY] because my heart feels like it's beating too fast.
Your airway is clear and patent. Any trouble breathing now?
You're breathing normally. I hear nice bilateral lung sounds. Any chest discomfort now or difficulty breathing with that rapid heart rate?
Radial pulse is regular and present-skin is normal, warm, and dry.
Pulse is at 92 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate is 22 breaths per minute. Just take deep and slow if you can now, okay?
Blood pressure is 159 over 89. That's elevated-how have you been managing your hypertension lately?
Oxygen saturation is 100%, which is great-any shortness of breath now or before you woke up?
Blood glucose initially measured as 114 milligrams per deciliter. We'll reassess later.
EKG is showing sinus rhythm. Would you like me to explain the findings briefly?
When exactly did this start today? Was it right when you woke up or a bit later?
Does the fast heart rate change when you move or breathe differently?
Can you describe the nature of the sensation-like rapid, irregular, or thumping?
Does it seem like this fast heartbeat is spreading to other parts of your body, like your arms or neck?
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate the discomfort from this fast heart rate?
Has the intensity changed at all since it started, and how does it feel now compared to a minute earlier?
Zero, really. It's not painful, it's just... fast and annoying.
In addition to the rapid heart rate, were there any other symptoms you noticed this morning before calling 911?
I had some mild shortness of breath at first, but that went by now.
Do you have any known drug or food allergies, Marjorie?
No known drug allergies. I generally react well to most things.
Are there any prescription or over-the-counter medications you're currently taking regularly?
I don't have any medications at this time.
Any past history of prior heart conditions or other serious medical problems we should know about?
None, just controlled hypertension.
What did you have to eat or drink before bed the previous night?
I went to sleep last night and just woke up like this. I didn't take anything before bed.
What were you doing when you first noticed your heart was beating fast?
I woke up and immediately just felt like it was racing. I panicked and called 911.
We're placing you on a 4-lead EKG monitor for ongoing cardiac assessment. Is that okay with you?
Yes, go ahead. That sounds okay to me.
4-lead monitoring is now active and transmitting. We'll proceed with a 12-lead EKG now.
Your skin is warm, dry, and shows no abnormal coloration.
Let's check your neurological functions. Are you aware of time, place, and people around you?
I sure am. I know where I am and who's here.
Great, and how many fingers am I holding up?
Three. You definitely don't have to ask that twice, you know.
Based on our findings and 12-lead EKG, we'll proceed with the 'chest pain-cardiac suspected' protocol for monitoring and hospital transport.
You mentioned wanting to go to [COMPANY_NAME]-that's where we are heading.
We'll now assist you onto the stretcher and secure you for transport.
Stretcher secured with harnesses and latching system in place. Transport is ready.
Your pulse has improved to 84 per minute now.
Your breathing is slower and more controlled now, at 18 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure remains elevated but stable, at 150 over 87 mmHg.
Oxygen saturation is still at 99%, which is excellent.
The EKG remains consistent in sinus rhythm, no new cardiac changes at this time.
By the way, have you recently tested positive for any infections or contagious illness, such as a recent virus?
Yes, I had a positive test for COVID three weeks ago, but I've felt fine ever since.
Transport is now secure and in progress.
Vital signs are steady. EKG is being monitored continuously during transit.
Patient reported feeling better and said, "Feeling better."
Has there been any nausea, vomiting, or stomach discomfort related to this rapid heart rate?
No, just the rapid heartbeat. Stomach feels fine.
I'm measuring your glucose again now-it came out as zero. Have you been eating normally or been without food for a long time?
I haven't had anything to eat yet today-could that be it?
It's possible. We'll monitor it as we go and check again at the hospital.
Final check of securing devices-harness, latching system, and side rails are fully engaged.
All set for transport, Marjorie. Let's make a final verbal check: you understand where you're going and what to expect during transit.
Yes, I understand. I feel better now, honestly.
Vital signs are within acceptable ranges and no acute change on EKG. We will continue with standard monitoring and proceed to [CITY].
No new medications or procedures are needed at this time.
Let's confirm your pain again-still no discomfort, with just a fast heart rate?
That's right, still zero pain and feeling somewhat better.
Given no change in symptoms, we'll log the current status into patient records and reiterate protocol as needed.
Let's just check once more-if you had a recent infection, are there any lingering effects to consider?
No, I haven't experienced any symptoms for three weeks now.
ED at [CITY] has been notified. Your vitals and EKG will be transmitted en route.
Stretchers and equipment are secure during movement. All team members are in position for transit.
Any sore throat, runny nose, or congestion today? Anything unusual?
No. It's been clear and I've felt fine aside from the heartbeat.
You are being transported in a position that promotes comfort. If you start to feel anything, immediately let me know.
I'll listen again for heart and lung sounds.
Sounds are equal and clear. Confirming initial findings.
We will continue to monitor you during transit. Everything is going as expected.
Same setup and medications-nothing new required.
I'm seeing no change in your glucose readings so far.
Still at zero. The hospital may consider supplements when you arrive.
Moving smoothly-no changes or obstacles-keep breathing as before.
If you feel anything out of the ordinary-even subtle-alert me as soon as you can.
Blood pressure remains 150 over 87; oxygen at 99%. No changes to note.
EKG readings have not changed. Showing ongoing sinus rhythm.
Your energy seems improved. No reported discomfort.
I can take part in normal conversation now. Thankful for timely help.
Any headaches, neck pain, or pressure you're experiencing while seated?
No, the head and neck feel fine, no pain.
Vehicle is pressurized and ready for travel. Standard procedure from here.
Moving your legs and feet now to check sensation and mobility-any limitations?
All okay. No numbness or sensations I don't usually have.
All restraints are double-checked for full coverage during acceleration.
Smooth and steady-Marjorie, I see you nodding-good, no complaints.
Just confirming our notes were thorough and accurate before final handover.
All findings remain consistent-nothing new to record.
